name: Dearg Due
description: The Dearg Due, meaning 'red blood sucker' or 'red woman,' is a fearsome creature from Irish folklore, a type of female vampire or evil fairy. Legend says she was once a beautiful woman forced into a cruel marriage by her family for financial gain. Driven to despair by the loveless union and the loss of her true love, she died under mysterious circumstances, some say of a broken heart, others by suicide. After her death, she rose again, fueled by vengeance and a thirst for the blood of men. Unlike the traditional vampire, the Dearg Due does not drain her victims completely, but rather saps their life force, leaving them weakened and vulnerable. She is said to lure men with her beauty, seducing them before draining their blood and life energy. She is a cautionary tale about forced marriages and the dark consequences of societal pressures.
subtitle: The Red Blood Sucker of Irish Legend
appearance: The Dearg Due is often described as an incredibly beautiful woman with long, flowing hair, typically red or black, and piercing eyes that can mesmerize her victims. She is said to have razor-sharp teeth, used for piercing the skin and draining blood. Her skin is pale, almost translucent, reflecting her undead nature. While she appears human, a closer look reveals an unnatural chill and an aura of malevolence.
habitat: Dearg Due are said to haunt the burial sites where they were originally interred, often old graveyards or ancient cairns. They may also be found near places of great sorrow or injustice, locations where they feel a connection to their past suffering. They are most active at night, particularly during the dark hours before dawn.
behavior: The Dearg Due is driven by vengeance and a thirst for blood. She actively seeks out men, using her beauty and charm to lure them into secluded locations. Once she has her victim alone, she drains their blood and life force. She is cunning and manipulative, preying on the vulnerabilities of her targets. She is also said to be fiercely protective of her territory and will attack anyone who threatens her domain.

keyMyths.title: The Dearg Due of Waterford
keyMyths.description: A tale tells of a Dearg Due who haunted the region near Waterford, Ireland. She would appear to lonely travelers on the road, leading them astray and draining their blood. Local lore says only a branch from a rowan tree could ward her off. The story highlights the Dearg Due's predatory nature and her association with specific locations.

keyMyths.title: The Forced Marriage
keyMyths.description: Many tales surrounding the Dearg Due emphasize the circumstances of her death. She was often forced into a loveless marriage against her will, leading to her demise. This tragic backstory fuels her vengeful spirit and her hatred of men. This is a common motif in stories about the Dearg Due, showcasing the societal pressures of the time. The betrayal and injustice of her situation are central to her myth.

keyMyths.title: The Cairns and Graveyards
keyMyths.description: Dearg Due are often associated with ancient burial sites, particularly cairns and graveyards. These locations are believed to be gateways to the otherworld, making them ideal places for supernatural entities to dwell. The Dearg Due's connection to these sites reinforces her status as a creature of the dead and a being connected to the ancient past. The association adds to her mysterious and terrifying nature.

keyMyths.title: The Rowan's Protection
keyMyths.description: In many accounts, the rowan tree is the only thing that can prevent the Dearg Due from draining your life force. Wielding a branch of a rowan tree will ward her off and protect the wielder from her powers. This reinforces the Rowan tree's reputation as a protective force against evil beings.

sources.author: Wilde, Lady
sources.work: Ancient Legends, Mystic Charms, and Superstitions of Ireland
sources.description: A collection of Irish folklore, including tales of fairies, ghosts, and other supernatural creatures. Provides context for understanding the Dearg Due within the broader framework of Irish beliefs.
sources.text: A collection of Irish folklore, including tales of fairies, ghosts, and other supernatural creatures. Provides context for understanding the Dearg Due within the broader framework of Irish beliefs.

sources.author: Lysaght, Patricia
sources.description: While focusing on the Banshee, this work provides valuable insights into Irish beliefs about death, the supernatural, and the role of female figures in folklore, which are relevant to understanding the Dearg Due.
sources.text: While focusing on the Banshee, this work provides valuable insights into Irish beliefs about death, the supernatural, and the role of female figures in folklore, which are relevant to understanding the Dearg Due.

sources.author: Briggs, Katharine Mary
sources.work: An Encyclopedia of Fairies, Hobgoblins, Brownies, Bogies, and Other Supernatural Creatures
sources.description: A comprehensive reference work on fairies and other supernatural beings from around the world, including entries on Irish fairies and related creatures, providing comparative context.
sources.text: A comprehensive reference work on fairies and other supernatural beings from around the world, including entries on Irish fairies and related creatures, providing comparative context.

sources.author: Gregory, Augusta
sources.work: Visions and Beliefs in the West of Ireland
sources.description: A collection of firsthand accounts of supernatural experiences from the West of Ireland, offering valuable insights into local beliefs and traditions related to fairies and other mythical beings, potentially including variations of the Dearg Due legend.
